tweets.text:
From the Week Ahead (https://t.co/dK8IKSbarp):

CNN Fear & Greed Index (blue line) remains a good distance from frothy levels edging up (the first gain in four weeks) to 60.2 from 58.6 the prior week, still down from 71.2 May 1st which was the highest since last July. The index remains in “Greed” but with still three indicators below Neutral (although still three in Extreme Greed):

Extreme Greed = market momentum (SPX vs 125-DMA); safe haven demand (20-day difference in stock/bond returns); put/call options (5-day put/call ratio)

Greed = None

Neutral = market volatility (VIX & its 50-DMA)

Fear = stock price strength (net new 52-week highs); stock price breadth (McClellan Volume Summation Index)

Extreme Fear = junk bond demand (vs investment grade) (from Fear)

GS: Why has Brent dropped 22% from its March peak despite Mideast tensions? 

Weak end demand (jet fuel, petchems, and China/Europe road fuels) and rapid destocking are offsetting supply shocks.

With 2mb/d of demand downside, 2026 Q4 price risks for Brent ($90) remain two-sided. https://t.co/fTj9hxOYyY

tweets.text:
As always, nice collection of posts from @C_Barraud.  One is the link to the Factset weekly earnings breakdown which notes that since the start of the quarter (April 1st) Q2 earnings expectations have seen an increase of +2.5%, the largest since Q3 2021 (+3.8%). 

That's in contrast to the 5-year average of a cut of -1.6% and the 10-year average of -2.2% (and Q1 was -1.4% (although Q3 and Q4 of 2025 saw small increases).

Most of this was due to a +59.2% surge for the Energy sector as well as a +5.6% boost to the heavyweight Tech sector. Materials was also above the SPX. 

On the other hand, six sectors recorded the typical decrease, easily led by the Health Care (-15.2%) sector. Industrials (-2.3%) was next.

Click through for the whole post with more charts as well as the other posts.

tweets.text:
Tier1Alpha with a similar take on passive flows pushing up the market:

Our 401(k) model continues to show consistent inflows into the target-date fund space, funneling roughly $33 billion into equities per month. In a market this concentrated, those flows can have an outsized impact at the single-stock level, as the incremental bid is increasingly directed into the largest names.

In fact, Nvidia alone is receiving roughly $2.87 billion in equity demand a month from these strategies, meaning that even after last week’s earnings event, 70 million working Americans will still be automatically buying NVDA every month, whether they realize it or not.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Citadel's Rubner highlights a point that @profplum99 has been talking about for years: "Passive vehicles are playing an increasingly dominant role in determining where marginal equity demand is allocated.

"Passive buying is not neutral in today’s market structure. Every $1 https://t.co/0EcybmGea3

tweets.text: Actually it does need explaining because you need to overlay wage and economic growth which is where the inflation comes from.  In the 70s, besides the structural issues, wages and economic growth were rising strongly which increased economic demand which pushed inflation.  Today, there is simply no comparison.  Inflation will always track economic and wages excluding temporary supply shocks (oil).

Goldman: We estimate IPO gross proceeds will total a record $225 billion in 2026. Our previous forecast was for $160 billion of IPO issuance. 

Although we expect a record year in 2026 for corporate equity issuance, supply relative to the size of the equity market should remain below the long-term average. Our estimate for $675 billion of corporate equity supply equates to 1% of Russell 3000 market cap, in line with average annual equity issuance from 2015-2019.

tweets.text:
And many have noted that it feels like the market rallies on the same recycled "peace agreement is imminent" headlines.  JPM's Fabio Bassi unfortunately didn't study the relationship of peace agreement headlines with Nasdaq, but he did look at it Nasdaq returns vs Brent and found "convexity optimism".

In other words, the Nasdaq rallies more on declines in Brent than it falls when Brent gains. 

“Convexity is more visible and statistical fits are materially stronger amid downside oil moves in comparison to upside ones since the start of the conflict,”  Bassi wrote, adding that the asymmetry “underpins” the market’s “buy the winners” narrative, reinforcing investors’ inclination to “lean into the assets that have demonstrated the strongest positive convexity to good-news catalysts."
tweets.full_text:

In other words, the Nasdaq rallies more on declines in Brent than it falls when Brent gains. 

“Convexity is more visible and statistical fits are materially stronger amid downside oil moves in comparison to upside ones since the start of the conflict,”  Bassi wrote, adding that the asymmetry “underpins” the market’s “buy the winners” narrative, reinforcing investors’ inclination to “lean into the assets that have demonstrated the strongest positive convexity to good-news catalysts."

tweets.text:
To the extent the fall in rates has been a boon to the market, BMO's awesome rates strategist Ian Lyngen notes the summer is kind to bonds historically: 

“It goes without saying that geopolitical developments and the traditional macro fundamentals will take precedence over any influence of the seasonals on the direction of US rates."

“Still the bond-bullish seasonality during the next phase of the year is something we’re keeping in mind as the market continues to contemplate the extent to which nominal rates will be able to push beyond the local extremes even if oil collapses on the announcement of a US-Iran peace deal.”

tweets.text:
Goldman: Speculative sentiment today appears far less extreme than at the ends of past overextended markets.

 For example, while retail trading activity has recently increased, it remains below historical and recent highs.

 Our Speculative Trading Indicator shows a similar pattern. 

Likewise, while the IPO calendar has been a major focus of recent investor conversations, capital markets activity has recently been light relative to past cycles. Although we expect a record year of issuance in 2026, we view that supply as a risk rather than a reason for pessimism, and expect corporate equity demand will outweigh supply this year.

tweets.text:
Stocks for the long run...

US stocks produced a 6.9% real return (closer to 5% for ex-US) 1802-2025
Bonds 3.3%
Bills 2.5%
Gold 0.9%
Oil 0.4% (since the early 1900s)
Dollar -1.5%

My take: commodities may be tempting, but their long-term returns are dreadful https://t.co/LdXZjJ7FjN

tweets.text:
BoA (Hartnett): US households held $68tn in equities in Dec’25, representing roughly 37% of US household net worth (a record high), up from $35tn in Dec’19).  

Using BofA GWIM equity holdings data, we calculate that US household equity wealth has risen by roughly $5tn YTD in 2026. This follows a $10tn gain in ’25, a $9tn gain in ’24, an $8tn gain in ’23, and a  $10tn loss in ’22.  

Massive equity gains & wealth gains have created a “boom-loop” ... the winners own stocks, the losers don’t.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: BoA (Hartnett): The stock of US private sector financial  assets (stocks & bonds, but also cash deposits, loans, private equity, and pension fund reserves) is [at a record high] relative to US GDP, illustrating the “financialization” of  the US economy and the high degree of https://t.co/252vMb1n2I

Using BofA GWIM equity holdings data, we calculate that US household equity wealth has risen by roughly $5tn YTD in 2026. This follows a $10tn gain in ’25, a $9tn gain in ’24, an $8tn gain in ’23, and a  $10tn loss in ’22.  

Massive equity gains & wealth gains have created a “boom-loop” ... the winners own stocks, the losers don’t.

tweets.text:
Tier1Alpha: SPX skew remains in outright free fall, approaching levels last seen in late 2024 after the post-election unwind.

However, that period proved short-lived, as the December Fed meeting triggered a nearly 3% decline in SPX and quickly reset demand for downside protection. 

Ultimately, when positioning gets this complacent this fast, it rarely resolves quietly.

tweets.text:
Yardeni: An earnings-led meltup like this should be more sustainable than a P/E-led meltup fueled by irrational exuberance. That’s especially true of fabulous earnings momentum (FEMO) meltups, like this one, that have been climbing a wall of worry.

The S&P 500 closed at a record 7,580.06 on Friday, 11.0% above its 200-day moving average (chart). The equal-weight index closed at 8,442.40, 7.2% above its 200-dma. Both are elevated and may pull back.

On May 10, we raised our year-end target for the S&P 500 from 7,700 to 8,250. We are sticking with it.
